title: Trust and Political Information: Attitudinal Change in Participants in the Youth Parliament in Brazil

tough_call_reason: The article explicitly invokes quasi-experimental logic and difference in difference, but the body text also states that the data do not allow a direct causal test of whether political information caused increased confidence. title: The Impacts of Conditional Cash Transfers in Four Presidential Elections (2002–2014)
causal_design_quote: This was done by matching beneficiaries to non-beneficiaries on gender, age, income, education, region, race (when available), and municipality's human development index (HDI-M) of respondents[3]. Matching was done using the genetic matching algorithm, developed by Diamond and Sekhon (2005).
tough_call_reason: The paper uses matching and generalized propensity score methods and explicitly frames estimates as BFP effects, supporting a credibility-revolution matching classification. However, it also acknowledges that Brazil lacks randomized pilots or sharp discontinuities and that the 2014 indirect-effect explanation is not directly tested, so causal attribution is qualified.

title: Impact of Electronic Voting Machines on Blank Votes and Null Votes in Brazilian Elections in 1998
causal_design_quote: A simple way to "isolate" the effects of cities' socioeconomic levels is to compare the average amount of null and blank votes from 1998 to those from the same cities in the previous elections (1994), a technique known as difference-in-differences, or diff-in-diff.
tough_call_reason: The article explicitly uses difference-in-differences as a causal design, but presents the results as average percentage-point differences without regression output or uncertainty estimates. I therefore classify it as statistical modeling for the quantitative-analysis type because of the explicit DiD design, while coding statistical inference as absent.
